body_txt: It can be hard to be a Housewife . You live in Stepford Suburbia . Your kids and husband need stuff from you all the time. Men Can't Keep House . You probably don't make or have your own money and may be at the mercy of your husband's Financial and/or Domestic Abuse . No wonder you need a mother's little helper to get through the day. In most cases, often to comply with network standards, the drug will go unnamed in order to pass as a G-Rated Drug . However, in more modern cases, the drug may be named as Valium, or as Paxil, Prozac, or other antidepressants. note Paxil and Prozac are actually SSRIs , meaning they don't have immediate or short-term effects outside of bad reactions. Instead they're taken daily, like blood pressure or cholesterol medication, and take effect gradually over a period of several weeks. They also aren't addictive in ways that involve cravings or abuse of the drug, though stopping them abruptly may cause physical symptoms. This was Truth in Television and written about in Betty Friedan's The Feminine Mystique ; however, it is far from being a Discredited Trope . More updated versions of this trope may take on the Predatory Big Pharma , though addictions to "mother's little helpers" will generally be played less seriously than if they belonged to men. While this trope may seem identical to the Junkie Parent , it is in fact a subtrope. The implications and deployment are vastly different. They have the same relationship as Alcoholic Parent to Lady Drunk (with which both may overlap, though the pill-abusing mother does not need to also abuse alcohol). While the Junkie Parent may be of any gender, the abuser of a mother's little helper is Always Female . She is white. She is middle-class, upper-middle-class, or upper-class, and almost never working-class or lower-class. She may be an Obnoxious Entitled Housewife . The more upper-class she is, the less likely it is that she does housework, but she may be similarly trapped in a Happy Marriage Charade with an abusive or cheating husband. She is portrayed as trying to uphold the standards of whatever time she lives in (however poorly she may be doing it). She is a Stepford Smiler , and her addiction is portrayed as part of why she can behave like this. She can be a completely Functional Addict and in Lighter and Softer works, it may be portrayed only as a Compressed Vice . None of these elements prevent her from becoming an Addled Addict in the end, but it is equally possible that she will simply be able to kick the addiction without the need for further help. With a greater understanding of addiction and more options for women beyond Stay in the Kitchen , it is moving into being a Dead Horse Trope outside of historical fiction, but it may still appear from time to time. Subtrope of Drug Based Characterization , where a character's needs and motivations are reflected by the drugs they take.
